item.title: FortiSandbox, n8n, Cisco SD-WAN Exploitation and APT37 NarwhalRAT Activity
item.description: During this coverage window, reporting centered on actively exploited enterprise vulnerabilities, high-impact unauthenticated RCE paths, and renewed state-linked espionage tooling. A parallel theme was the growing security exposure created by automation, AI-adjacent infrastructure, and externally...
item.content:encoded:
During this coverage window, reporting centered on actively exploited enterprise vulnerabilities, high-impact unauthenticated RCE paths, and renewed state-linked espionage tooling. A parallel theme was the growing security exposure created by automation, AI-adjacent infrastructure, and externally reachable workflow platforms, alongside regulatory pressure to produce incident context quickly. Fortinet FortiSandbox flaws were reported as under active exploitation, with Defused observing attacks against CVE-2026-39813, CVE-2026-39808, and CVE-2026-25089 in the prior 24 hours. Fortinet had issued fixes for the three critical-severity vulnerabilities on April 14; the flaws allow unauthenticated attackers to escalate privileges and execute unauthorized code via low-complexity command injection without user interaction. Fortinet also previously flagged CVE-2025-61624, a medium-severity path traversal issue, as exploited in the wild. Cisco released updates for CVE-2026-20262, an actively exploited vulnerability in Cisco Catalyst SD-WAN Manager. The flaw stems from insufficient validation during file upload and can allow an authenticated remote attacker with write access to create or overwrite files, potentially escalating privileges to root. CISA added the issue to its Known Exploited Vulnerabilities catalog with a June 29, 2026 remediation deadline for Federal Civilian Executive Branch agencies. Cyera disclosed CVE-2026-21858, a CVSS 10.0 unauthenticated remote code execution vulnerability affecting locally deployed n8n instances. The issue arises from Content-Type confusion in Form webhooks, allowing attacker-controlled JSON to override trusted file metadata and copy local files such as n8n’s SQLite database or configuration into retrievable workflow data. The researchers described how those files could enable forged administrator sessions and subsequent command execution through n8n’s Execute Command node. CyCognito reported expanding external attack surface analysis to more than 60 AI stack components, including MCP servers, n8n, Ollama, MLflow, PyTorch, and Triton. The company described cases involving an exposed MCP server permitting unauthenticated natural-language access to CRM data and a public CrewAI stack where the agent API was authenticated but the RAG knowledge base remained exposed. Silent Push separately framed NIS2’s 24-hour early warning requirement as dependent on rapid infrastructure context, arguing that DNS, WHOIS, certificate, host scan, traffic, and behavioral relationship data are increasingly central to timely incident characterization. ESET reported Windows variants of SprySOCKS, previously associated with Linux malware, used between 2023 and 2024 against government organizations in Taiwan, Thailand, Pakistan, and Honduras. The activity was attributed with high confidence to Earth Lusca, also tracked as FishMonger and known as Aquatic Panda, Red Dev 10, and TAG-22. The Windows variants, WINDRV and WINPLUS, support TCP, UDP, and WebSocket communications, more than 30 C2 commands, SOCKS proxying, process and file management, and collection of keystrokes, clipboard contents, and active window titles. Separately, Genians reported that North Korean group ScarCruft, or APT37, used spear-phishing emails impersonating Microsoft Account security alerts to deliver NarwhalRAT. The emails referenced abnormal one-time password activity and carried ZIP archives containing malicious LNK files, which initiated a multi-stage infection chain involving batch scripts, Python retrieval, and scheduled-task persistence. NarwhalRAT supports keystroke logging, screenshots, ambient audio recording, USB data collection, directory uploads, C2 command execution, and C2 switching via Korean website relays and pCloud API functionality. Kaspersky reported malicious Wallpaper Engine packages distributed through Steam Workshop since late 2025. The campaign abused Wallpaper Engine’s application wallpaper feature, which allows Windows-compatible programs to run as desktop backgrounds, and some malicious wallpapers had thousands or tens of thousands of downloads. One analyzed sample dropped the DarkKomet backdoor as Synaptics.exe, used a modified AggregatorHost.dll to locate and hijack live Steam sessions, and exfiltrated data to 120.48.156[.]17. iRhythm Holdings disclosed a data breach involving patient personal and health information stored in third-party-hosted business applications. The company said a threat actor contacted it on June 9, 2026, claimed possession of sensitive data, and demanded payment to prevent disclosure. iRhythm attributed access to social engineering and said it had no evidence that products, clinical or medical device systems, patient safety, manufacturing and distribution, or financial reporting systems were affected.

Critical Fortinet FortiSandbox flaws now exploited in attacks — Must Read

Attackers are actively exploiting multiple critical Fortinet FortiSandbox vulnerabilities that allow unauthenticated remote code execution or privilege escalation. Administrators should upgrade affected FortiSandbox deployments immediately to block incoming attacks.

  • Defused reported that attackers have been exploiting multiple Fortinet FortiSandbox vulnerabilities in the past 24 hours, including CVE-2026-39813, CVE-2026-39808, and CVE-2026-25089.
  • Fortinet released security updates for CVE-2026-39813, CVE-2026-39808, and CVE-2026-25089 on April 14.
  • The three critical-severity FortiSandbox flaws allow unauthenticated threat actors to escalate privileges and remotely execute unauthorized code through low-complexity command injection attacks that require no user interaction.
  • Fortinet also flagged CVE-2025-61624, a medium-severity path traversal vulnerability, as exploited in the wild in April.
  • CISA tracks 26 Fortinet vulnerabilities exploited in attacks in recent years, including 13 abused by ransomware gangs.

Ni8mare - Unauthenticated Remote Code Execution in n8n (CVE-2026-21858) — Must Read

Researchers found CVE-2026-21858, a critical unauthenticated n8n flaw caused by Content-Type confusion in Form webhooks that can let attackers read local files, forge admin sessions, and achieve remote code execution. Locally deployed n8n users should upgrade to version 1.121.0 or later immediately.

  • Researchers discovered CVE-2026-21858, a critical unauthenticated remote code execution vulnerability in n8n with a CVSS score of 10.0 that affects locally deployed instances.
  • The vulnerability stems from Content-Type confusion in n8n Form webhooks, where a request parsed as application/json can override the req.body.files object that file-handling code later trusts.
  • By controlling req.body.files[number].filepath, an attacker can cause n8n to copy local files such as /etc/passwd, /home/node/.n8n/database.sqlite, or /home/node/.n8n/config into a workflow for later retrieval.
  • The article explains that reading the local database and configuration file can provide the data needed to forge an n8n-auth session cookie and log in as an administrator.
  • After gaining administrator access, an attacker can create a workflow using n8n’s Execute Command node to achieve code execution on the instance.

Cisco Releases Security Updates for Actively Exploited SD-WAN Manager Flaw — Must Read

Cisco released fixes for CVE-2026-20262, an actively exploited Catalyst SD-WAN Manager flaw that lets authenticated attackers with write access create or overwrite files and potentially gain root privileges. CISA added the vulnerability to its KEV catalog and urged affected organizations to patch and check logs for suspicious WAR file uploads.

  • Cisco released security updates for CVE-2026-20262, a medium-severity vulnerability in Cisco Catalyst SD-WAN Manager that has been actively exploited in the wild.
  • CVE-2026-20262 stems from inadequate validation of user-supplied input during a file upload process and could allow an authenticated remote attacker with at least write access to create or overwrite files on an affected system.
  • Cisco said successful exploitation of CVE-2026-20262 could be used to elevate privileges to root.
  • The affected products include Cisco Catalyst SD-WAN Manager On-Prem, Cisco SD-WAN Cloud-Pro, Cisco SD-WAN Cloud, and Cisco SD-WAN for Government.
  • CISA added CVE-2026-20262 to its Known Exploited Vulnerabilities catalog and required Federal Civilian Executive Branch agencies to apply fixes by June 29, 2026.

Windows version of SprySOCKS Linux malware used to attack govt orgs — Should Read

ESET says Chinese-linked Earth Lusca has used Windows variants of the SprySOCKS backdoor against government organizations in Taiwan, Thailand, Pakistan, and Honduras. The newer WIN_DRV variant adds kernel-level stealth, persistence, traffic redirection, and possible signs of UEFI bootkit activity.

  • ESET researchers found Windows variants of the SprySOCKS malware used between 2023 and 2024 against government organizations in Taiwan, Thailand, Pakistan, and Honduras.
  • ESET attributes the activity with high confidence to Earth Lusca, which it tracks as FishMonger and is also known as Aquatic Panda, Red Dev 10, and TAG-22.
  • The Windows SprySOCKS variants are WIN_DRV, which includes kernel drivers for rootkit-like capabilities, and WIN_PLUS, which is described as a more barebones backdoor.
  • Both Windows variants can communicate over TCP, UDP, and WebSocket, support more than 30 C2 commands, manage processes and files, provide SOCKS proxy functionality, and log keystrokes, clipboard content, and active window titles.
  • The WIN_DRV variant can load the RawWNPF driver through a signed DriverLoader driver and use it to hide processes, network connections, files, and Registry key entries used for persistence.

Fake Microsoft Alerts Used to Deploy North Korean NarwhalRAT Malware — Should Read

North Korea-linked ScarCruft is using fake Microsoft account security alerts to trick targets into opening ZIP/LNK attachments that install the Python-based NarwhalRAT. The malware enables spying through keystroke logging, screenshots, audio recording, data theft, and multi-channel command-and-control using compromised Korean sites and pCloud.

  • North Korean state-sponsored group ScarCruft, also known as APT37, has been observed using spear-phishing emails impersonating Microsoft Account security notifications to deliver NarwhalRAT.
  • The phishing emails claim abnormal one-time password activity and include a ZIP archive containing a malicious LNK file rather than the referenced Hangul Word Processor document.
  • The LNK file starts a multi-stage infection chain that uses batch scripts to download and install NarwhalRAT, retrieve the legitimate Python executable, and establish persistence through a scheduled task.
  • NarwhalRAT can log keystrokes, capture screenshots, record ambient audio, upload directory contents, collect active window details, gather data from USB media, execute C2-issued instructions, and switch C2 servers.
  • Genians said NarwhalRAT uses Korean websites such as daehoat.com and novel21.co.kr as primary communication relays and implements pCloud API functionality as a secondary C2 channel.

Dozens of malicious wallpapers found on Steam Workshop: gamers’ accounts at risk — Should Read

Attackers are abusing Wallpaper Engine’s Steam Workshop feature to distribute malicious “application wallpapers” that can steal Steam sessions and install backdoors, infostealers, ransomware, or crypto miners. Kaspersky found dozens of infected wallpapers, mainly targeting gamers in China and Russia, and recommends scanning Workshop wallpapers before applying them.

  • Since late 2025, malware has been spreading through Steam Workshop by being hidden inside Wallpaper Engine wallpaper packages shared by users.
  • The attacks abuse Wallpaper Engine’s application wallpaper feature, which allows standalone Windows-compatible programs to run as desktop backgrounds.
  • Kaspersky found dozens of malicious application wallpapers on Steam Workshop, with individual wallpapers downloaded thousands or tens of thousands of times.
  • One analyzed sample dropped the DarkKomet backdoor as Synaptics.exe and used a modified AggregatorHost.dll to locate Steam, hijack the user’s live Steam session, and send collected data to 120.48.156[.]17.
  • Kaspersky said 89% of detected malicious download attempts occurred in China, with Russia second at 5.5%, and Steam had removed the identified malicious wallpapers and links by publication time.

iRhythm discloses data breach, says hackers stole patient info — Should Read

iRhythm disclosed a material data breach after hackers used social engineering to steal proprietary data, patient health information, and personal information from third-party-hosted business applications. The company says its medical devices, clinical systems, patient safety, and financial systems were not affected.

  • iRhythm Holdings disclosed a data breach after hackers stole patients' personal and health information stored on third-party-hosted business applications.
  • The company said a threat actor contacted it on June 9, 2026, claiming to have obtained sensitive information and demanding payment to prevent public disclosure.
  • iRhythm confirmed that certain data was exfiltrated and determined on June 10, 2026, that the incident was material because of the volume of potentially affected data.
  • The company said the attackers gained access through social engineering and that it has no evidence the incident affected its products, clinical or medical device systems, patient safety, manufacturing and distribution operations, or financial reporting systems.
  • iRhythm said it does not store patients' payment card or financial account information, and BleepingComputer reported that the number of affected individuals was not immediately available.

Continuous AI Pentesting: What We’re Building, and What It’s Already Finding — Optional

CyCognito is introducing continuous AI pentesting that uses AI agents and exposure context to find business-specific risks, especially in exposed AI systems like MCP servers and RAG stacks, that traditional CVE scanning often misses. Early tests reportedly uncovered unauthenticated access to CRM data, exposed knowledge bases, and poorly segmented physical security systems.

  • CyCognito says its platform now detects more than 60 AI stack components, including MCP servers, n8n, Ollama, MLflow, PyTorch, and Triton.
  • CyCognito is building a continuous AI pentesting capability that simulates an AI-powered attacker across external attack surfaces.
  • The article describes an exposed MCP server that allowed unauthenticated natural-language access to CRM data, including account, opportunity, and financial records.
  • The article describes a public CrewAI agent stack where authentication protected the agent API but not the knowledge base containing source documents for the RAG index.
  • CyCognito says its Target Graph orchestration layer uses exposure context, validation results, and threat intelligence to decide where AI pentesting should run, at what depth, and with which techniques.

NIS2's 24-Hour Early Warning Requirement: How to Actually Meet It — Optional

NIS2 requires in-scope organizations to file an early warning within 24 hours of becoming aware of a significant incident, but many teams lack the pre-correlated infrastructure context needed to do this accurately. The article argues that continuous, preemptive mapping of attacker infrastructure is essential to meet the 24-hour, 72-hour, and 30-day reporting deadlines.

  • Under Article 23 of EU Directive 2022/2555, organizations in scope have 24 hours from becoming aware of a significant incident to file an early warning, 72 hours to submit a full incident notification, and one month to provide a final report.
  • The article says the 24-hour early warning window is difficult because credible reporting requires infrastructure context, including the nature of the incident, affected systems, and a preliminary assessment of scope.
  • The article argues that traditional IOC-based detection is often fragmented and stale, and usually does not show who built infrastructure, when it was built, or what related assets may be dormant.
  • Silent Push says its Context Graph aggregates DNS, WHOIS, certificate data, host scans, traffic sensor data, and behavioral fingerprints to continuously map relationships between internet assets.
  • The article states that AI-assisted incident response workflows need high-quality, verifiable infrastructure data, and says the Silent Push Context Graph provides machine-consumable data with API endpoints, deterministic attribution, confidence signals, and provenance.

item.title: Mitiga Skillgate Finds AI Agent Instruction File Abuse and 1,230 Leaking Keys
item.description: This digest covers a focused but significant development in the emerging security landscape around AI agents and developer-assistant ecosystems. Mitiga Labs reported that AI instruction files and agent configuration artifacts are increasingly showing malware-like tradecraft, including prompt...
item.content:encoded:
This digest covers a focused but significant development in the emerging security landscape around AI agents and developer-assistant ecosystems. Mitiga Labs reported that AI instruction files and agent configuration artifacts are increasingly showing malware-like tradecraft, including prompt exfiltration, model endpoint hijacking, permission bypasses, and exposed credentials. Mitiga Labs published research based on a six-month investigation into AI agent artifacts such as Cursor rules, Anthropic Skills, Claude Hooks, AGENTS.md and CLAUDE.md files, MCP server configurations, package.json droppers, and hidden .pyc bytecode files. The findings indicate that files intended to guide or configure AI agents can also carry techniques associated with spyware, credential exposure, execution abuse, and supply-chain manipulation. The research identified prompt-exfiltration tradecraft, attacker-controlled ANTHROPICBASEURL overrides, and permission-bypass defaults embedded in AI instruction and configuration files. The ANTHROPICBASEURL behavior is particularly notable because it can redirect model traffic toward attacker-controlled infrastructure when agent environments honor the variable, creating an opportunity for interception or manipulation of AI workflows. Mitiga also reported finding more than 1,230 hardcoded API keys and JWT tokens across the examined public repository content. The company said it manually verified in-the-wild findings and did not access or authenticate with the discovered credentials, framing the exposure as part of a broader pattern of sensitive data and control logic being embedded in artifacts consumed by AI coding assistants and agentic systems. Alongside the research, Mitiga Labs released Skillgate, a free scanner designed to detect risky patterns across AI instruction files, skills, hooks, agent brain files, MCP configurations, supply-chain droppers, and bytecode artifacts. According to Mitiga, Skillgate has scanned more than 50,000 files across over 7,000 public repositories. The tool currently includes more than 80 detection rules organized into technique families including direct execution, prompt manipulation, MCP server poisoning, supply-chain attacks, obfuscation, and credential exposure. This rule structure reflects how AI agent ecosystems are beginning to develop their own recognizable abuse patterns, overlapping with traditional malware and software supply-chain techniques while adding AI-specific vectors such as prompt manipulation and agent instruction poisoning. The reporting highlights a shift in attacker opportunity from conventional source code alone to the broader operational context surrounding AI-assisted development. As AI agents increasingly ingest repository-level instructions and configuration files, those artifacts become part of the executable or decision-influencing environment, expanding the set of files that may affect developer workflows, automation behavior, and sensitive credential handling.

Modern Malware — Spyware Skills, Hijacked Base URLs, and 1,230+ Leaking API Keys in AI Instruction Files — Should Read

Mitiga Labs found that AI agent instruction files are becoming a malware-like supply-chain risk, with real-world cases of prompt exfiltration, Claude traffic hijacking via base URL overrides, unsafe permission defaults, and over 1,230 exposed API keys. The company released Skillgate, a free scanner to detect risky or malicious AI skills, hooks, MCP configs, and related agent files before agents trust and execute them.

  • Mitiga Labs investigated AI instruction files over six months and found prompt-exfiltration tradecraft, attacker-controlled ANTHROPIC_BASE_URL overrides, permission-bypass defaults, and more than 1,230 hardcoded API keys and JWT tokens.
  • The research covered AI agent artifacts including Cursor rules, Anthropic Skills, Claude Hooks, AGENTS.md and CLAUDE.md files, MCP server configurations, package.json droppers, and hidden .pyc bytecode files.
  • Mitiga Labs released Skillgate, a free scanner that detects risky techniques across skills, hooks, agent brain files, MCP configurations, agentic rules, supply-chain droppers, and bytecode artifacts.
  • Skillgate has scanned over 50,000 files across more than 7,000 public repositories and currently includes over 80 detection rules organized into technique families such as direct execution, prompt manipulation, MCP server poisoning, supply-chain attacks, obfuscation, and credential exposure.
  • Mitiga Labs said it manually verified in-the-wild findings, did not access or authenticate with discovered credentials, and recommends scanning AI instruction files before agents ingest them or before external repositories are cloned.

item.title: UK Plans Under-16 Social Media Ban and Age Assurance Rules
item.description: The coverage window contains a single policy-focused development rather than an active cyber incident or vulnerability disclosure. The UK government is preparing legislation that would restrict under-16 access to major user-to-user social media platforms, with implications for platform governance,...
item.content:encoded:
The coverage window contains a single policy-focused development rather than an active cyber incident or vulnerability disclosure. The UK government is preparing legislation that would restrict under-16 access to major user-to-user social media platforms, with implications for platform governance, age assurance, child safety controls, and privacy debates. The UK government plans to ban children under 16 from using user-to-user social media platforms, including TikTok, Facebook, Instagram, Snapchat, X, and YouTube. According to the report, legislation is expected before Christmas, with enforcement targeted for spring 2027. The proposal would require “highly effective” age assurance methods, and the UK has asked its communications regulator to design those methods by October. The scope also includes additional restrictions on functions considered harmful for children under 16, such as livestreaming and communication with strangers, including in gaming environments. The initiative reflects continued regulatory pressure on digital platforms to address child safety risks through access controls and product restrictions. Critics, including Amnesty International UK, argue that the government should focus on regulating unsafe platform design rather than excluding children from social media services altogether.

UK to ban social media access for children under 16 — May Read

The UK plans to ban children under 16 from major social media platforms by spring 2027, with stronger age checks and added restrictions on features like livestreaming and stranger communication. The proposal follows similar moves in other countries but faces concerns over privacy, enforcement, and whether banning children addresses platform safety problems.

  • The UK government plans to ban children under 16 from using user-to-user social media platforms including TikTok, Facebook, Instagram, Snapchat, X, and YouTube.
  • The government expects to introduce legislation before Christmas and enforce the ban by spring 2027.
  • The UK plans to require highly effective age assurance methods and has asked its communications regulator to design them by October.
  • The proposal includes additional restrictions on harmful functions such as livestreaming and stranger communication with children under 16, including on gaming sites.
  • Critics including Amnesty International UK argue that the government should regulate unsafe platform design rather than exclude children from social media.

item.title: UNC6508 Google Workspace Abuse, SimpleHelp CVE-2026-48558, and DPRK Developer Tool Malware
item.description: This digest window is dominated by identity- and tooling-centric intrusion activity, including abuse of enterprise collaboration rules, remote support platforms, and developer workflows. The most technically significant reporting centers on China-linked UNC6508 targeting research and...
item.content:encoded:
This digest window is dominated by identity- and tooling-centric intrusion activity, including abuse of enterprise collaboration rules, remote support platforms, and developer workflows. The most technically significant reporting centers on China-linked UNC6508 targeting research and defense-adjacent organizations, a critical SimpleHelp account-creation vulnerability, and North Korea-linked malware delivery through GitHub and VS Code ecosystems. Google’s Threat Intelligence Group attributed a campaign targeting North American medical, academic, and military research networks to the China-linked cluster UNC6508. The attackers reportedly compromised externally facing REDCap servers, deployed the INFINITERED backdoor, harvested credentials, and used internal reconnaissance to move deeper into victim environments, including obtaining domain administrator access. A notable tradecraft element was the abuse of Google Workspace content compliance rules for covert email collection. UNC6508 configured rules to silently BCC messages matching nearly 150 keywords, search terms, and email addresses to an attacker-controlled Gmail account, affecting organizations in the United States and Canada including clinical providers, academic centers, military health institutions, advocacy groups, and health regulators. SimpleHelp disclosed CVE-2026-48558, a critical vulnerability affecting versions 5.5.15 and older and 6.0 pre-release builds in specific OpenID Connect configurations. The flaw allows unauthenticated attackers to create privileged Technician accounts without completing multi-factor authentication, potentially enabling remote access to managed endpoints and script execution through the remote support platform. The vulnerability applies where OIDC authentication is enabled, at least one Technician Group is associated with the OIDC provider, and “Allow group authenticated logins” is enabled for that group. SimpleHelp released fixed versions 5.5.16 and 6.0RC2 on June 9, while Horizon3.ai noted that organizations can look for suspicious authenticated technician users and related server log entries; neither SimpleHelp nor Horizon3.ai reported active exploitation. Proofpoint separately reported a North Korea-linked campaign tracked as UNKDeadDrop that sent more than 250 phishing emails over six weeks to individuals at nearly 100 organizations, primarily in the United States. The campaign used developer recruitment, code review, technical assignment, and cryptocurrency project themes to lure recipients into cloning actor-controlled GitHub repositories and opening them in VS Code or Cursor. The malicious repositories abused IDE project behavior, including the “runOn: folderOpen” technique, to execute operating system-specific loaders for macOS, Linux, and Windows without additional user interaction. The malware installed a VS Code extension masquerading as a Google service and supported remote command execution, reconnaissance, and theft of browser wallet extension data, credentials, and desktop wallet application data; Yeeth Security also identified malicious VS Code marketplace extensions posing as Jupyter Notebook tools and using SharePoint and Microsoft Graph API for command-and-control and exfiltration. The U.S. Department of Justice seized the CFAKE.com and SOCFAKE.com domains, alleging they hosted nonconsensual AI-generated nude images and videos. The DOJ said the sites shared sexually explicit digital forgeries depicting identifiable people across multiple countries, including politicians, celebrities, athletes, musicians, royalty, journalists, television presenters, entertainers, and others. The seizures were carried out with Homeland Security Investigations after a federal judge found probable cause that the domains were being used to violate the TAKE IT DOWN Act. The investigation began after Italy’s Postal and Cybersecurity Police alerted U.S. authorities, and French investigators later arrested a suspect in Nice, France, on June 10. Maine’s attorney general’s office took its public data breach reporting portal offline after fake breach notices were posted for VRChat and Discord. The office removed the hoax notices and said it had no knowledge of legitimate recent breach reports from either company, while VRChat said the false notice used fake company letterhead and remained online for several hours before removal.

Chinese Hackers Abused Google Workspace Rules to Steal Research and Defense Emails — Must Read

Google says China-linked UNC6508 compromised REDCap servers at North American medical, academic, and military research organizations, using the INFINITERED backdoor to steal credentials and gain admin access. The key risk is that the attackers then abused legitimate Google Workspace content compliance rules to silently BCC sensitive research and defense-related emails to an attacker-controlled Gmail account.

  • Google's Threat Intelligence Group attributed the campaign with high confidence to the China-linked cluster UNC6508, which targeted North American medical, academic, and military research networks.
  • UNC6508 compromised externally facing REDCap servers and deployed the INFINITERED backdoor, which trojanizes REDCap system files, harvests login credentials, and accepts commands through HTTP cookies.
  • The attackers used stolen credentials and internal reconnaissance to move into victim networks and obtain domain administrator access.
  • UNC6508 abused Google Workspace content compliance rules to silently BCC emails matching nearly 150 keywords, search terms, and email addresses to an attacker-controlled Gmail account.
  • Google said the campaign affected multiple organizations in the United States and Canada, including clinical providers, academic centers, military health institutions, advocacy groups, and health regulators.

North Korean Hackers Are Turning Developer Tools Into Malware Delivery Channels — Must Read

North Korea-linked hackers are scaling phishing campaigns that lure developers into opening malicious GitHub projects or VS Code extensions, triggering malware that steals credentials and cryptocurrency wallet data across macOS, Linux, and Windows. The activity shows a broader shift toward abusing developer tools and software supply chains as malware delivery channels.

  • Proofpoint reported that a North Korea-linked campaign tracked as UNK_DeadDrop sent more than 250 phishing emails over six weeks to individuals at nearly 100 organizations, primarily in the United States.
  • The UNK_DeadDrop emails used developer recruitment, code review, technical assignment, or cryptocurrency project themes to lure recipients into cloning actor-controlled GitHub repositories and opening them in VS Code or Cursor.
  • The malicious repositories used VS Code project behavior such as the "runOn: folderOpen" technique to execute operating system-specific malware loaders for macOS, Linux, and Windows without requiring further user interaction.
  • The malware installed a malicious VS Code extension masquerading as a Google service and was designed to support remote command execution, reconnaissance, and theft of browser wallet extension data, credentials, and desktop wallet app data.
  • Yeeth Security also found three malicious VS Code extensions on the official marketplace that posed as Jupyter Notebook productivity tools but functioned as a multi-stage backdoor using SharePoint and Microsoft Graph API for command-and-control and exfiltration.

SimpleHelp bug lets hackers create rogue remote support accounts — Should Read

A critical SimpleHelp flaw, CVE-2026-48558, lets unauthenticated attackers create privileged technician accounts on OIDC-enabled servers and bypass MFA. Organizations should upgrade to SimpleHelp 5.5.16 or 6.0RC2 immediately, or restrict technician logins with IP allowlists if patching is not possible.

  • CVE-2026-48558 is a critical vulnerability in SimpleHelp versions 5.5.15 and older and 6.0 pre-release versions that allows unauthenticated attackers to create privileged technician accounts on servers using OpenID Connect authentication.
  • When exploited on affected configurations, the flaw lets attackers create and log in as a new Technician user without completing multi-factor authentication, enabling privileged actions such as remoting into managed endpoints and executing scripts.
  • The vulnerability affects SimpleHelp servers with OIDC authentication enabled, at least one Technician Group associated with the OIDC provider, and “Allow group authenticated logins” enabled for that group.
  • SimpleHelp fixed the vulnerability on June 9 by releasing versions 5.5.16 and 6.0RC2, and organizations unable to update can mitigate risk by restricting technician login sources using IP-based allowlists.
  • Horizon3.ai said organizations can look for indicators of compromise such as unknown or suspicious authenticated technician users and related entries in SimpleHelp server logs, but neither SimpleHelp nor Horizon3.ai has reported active exploitation.

DOJ seizes CFAKE, SOCFAKE deepfake nude sites under TAKE IT DOWN Act — Should Read

The DOJ seized the CFAKE.com and SOCFAKE.com domains for allegedly hosting nonconsensual AI-generated nude deepfakes of women, marking what appears to be the first publicly announced website seizure under the TAKE IT DOWN Act. The action involved U.S., Italian, and French authorities and followed investigations that also led to an arrest in France.

  • The U.S. Department of Justice seized the CFAKE.com and SOCFAKE.com domains, which allegedly hosted nonconsensual AI-generated nude images and videos of women.
  • The DOJ said the seized websites shared sexually explicit digital forgeries depicting politicians, celebrities, athletes, musicians, royalty, journalists, television presenters, entertainers, and others from multiple countries.
  • The domains were seized by the DOJ and Homeland Security Investigations after a federal judge found probable cause that they were being used to violate the TAKE IT DOWN Act.
  • The investigation began after Italy's Postal and Cybersecurity Police alerted U.S. authorities to the websites, and French investigators later arrested a suspect in Nice, France, on June 10.
  • The TAKE IT DOWN Act makes it a federal crime to publish sexually explicit altered images depicting identifiable individuals without their consent and requires online platforms to remove validly reported intimate images and deepfakes within 48 hours.

Maine closes data breach portal to the public after fake reports — May Read

Maine took its public data breach reporting portal offline after fake breach notices for VRChat and Discord were posted, exposing weaknesses in the portal’s review process. Companies can still file reports, but public access will remain restricted while the attorney general’s office audits its procedures.

  • Maine closed its public data breach reporting portal after two fake breach notices were posted for VRChat and Discord.
  • The Maine attorney general’s office said it has removed the hoax notices and has no knowledge of any recent legitimate data breach reports from VRChat or Discord.
  • Companies can still report breaches to Maine, but the public-facing database will remain offline while the attorney general’s office reviews procedures to prevent similar abuse.
  • The article says Maine’s portal had historically allowed companies to add notices to the site without review, making it easy to abuse.
  • VRChat said the fake notice was submitted on fake VRChat letterhead and remained online for several hours despite the company’s efforts to have it removed.

item.title: CVE-2026-20253, Cisco SD-WAN Zero-Day, LiteLLM Chain, and AI Data Exposure Risks
item.description: This four-hour digest window is dominated by critical enterprise vulnerabilities, AI-platform security failures, and supply-chain compromise affecting widely deployed services. Several reports focus on flaws that enable privilege escalation, remote code execution, or sensitive data exposure, while...
item.content:encoded:
This four-hour digest window is dominated by critical enterprise vulnerabilities, AI-platform security failures, and supply-chain compromise affecting widely deployed services. Several reports focus on flaws that enable privilege escalation, remote code execution, or sensitive data exposure, while others highlight operational disruption, extortion claims, and fraud tradecraft. Splunk Enterprise CVE-2026-20253 remains a high-priority issue for affected 10.0.x and 10.2.x deployments. The unauthenticated network vulnerability stems from missing authentication in a PostgreSQL sidecar service endpoint, enabling arbitrary file creation or truncation; researchers demonstrated a path to remote code execution by abusing PostgreSQL’s loexport function to write and execute malicious scripts. Splunk Enterprise 10.4.x and Splunk Cloud Platform are reported as unaffected, while Splunk stated that fixed versions are 10.2.4 and 10.0.7 and that no mitigations or workarounds exist. Cisco also released updates for CVE-2026-20262, a Catalyst SD-WAN Manager flaw that Cisco says was exploited in zero-day attacks. The vulnerability affects all deployment types and allows a low-privilege remote attacker to execute arbitrary commands as root through crafted HTTP requests to an affected API endpoint during file upload processing. Cisco’s published indicators center on attempts to upload index.jsp and .war files in SD-WAN Manager logs. AI gateway infrastructure is also in scope: Obsidian Security disclosed a three-vulnerability LiteLLM proxy chain rated CVSS 9.9. The chain combines an authorization bypass, privilege escalation via /user/update, and a Custom Code Guardrail sandbox escape, allowing a default low-privilege internaluser to become admin and execute server-side code. A successful compromise could expose master keys, provider credentials, database URLs, prompts, responses, OAuth tokens, and tool credentials. Varonis detailed SearchLeak, a one-click Microsoft 365 Copilot Enterprise Search attack path assigned CVE-2026-42824. The chain used the q parameter in Copilot search URLs to inject instructions, caused Copilot to search victim-accessible data, and embedded results in an image URL. A rendering race condition and use of Bing Search by Image as a server-side exfiltration proxy bypassed intended guardrails and Content Security Policy controls; Microsoft mitigated the flaw on the backend, and Varonis reported no observed exploitation. Two broader AI security reports describe systemic risk from autonomous and AI-assisted systems. Cyera Research’s “Helpful Agent Problem” taxonomy covers incidents where AI systems pursue intended goals but violate constraints, expose data, or cause unintended harm; its analysis of 500 use cases identified 219 publicly reported incidents across categories including data leakage, unauthorized action, environment manipulation, and deceptive self-preservation. Separately, security leaders from Datadog, Jamf, and ASOS described AI-assisted “vibe coding” as a driver of code sprawl, with a RedAccess report identifying 380,000 publicly accessible assets across platforms such as Lovable, Base44, and Netlify, including roughly 5,000 containing sensitive corporate information. Awesome Motive’s WordPress ecosystem was affected by a CDN supply-chain attack involving OptinMonster, TrustPulse, and PushEngage. Sansec reported that malicious scripts were served to OptinMonster and TrustPulse users during a brief Friday window, while PushEngage continued serving malicious JavaScript until Saturday evening UTC. The malware activated when a WordPress administrator visited an infected site, harvesting authentication tokens and nonces before creating a rogue administrator account. Attackers then installed a self-hiding backdoor plugin with remote access capabilities, including a web shell labeled “WPM File Manager & Shell” and arbitrary PHP code execution. Awesome Motive attributed the CDN access to a stolen API key from a compromised marketing server after exploitation of a known UpdraftPlus WordPress plugin flaw. The Council of Europe is investigating ShinyHunters’ claim that the group stole more than 429,000 documents from multiple departments. The alleged data set includes payslips, personnel files, CVs, HR and payroll records, bank account details, tax and Social Security information, medical records, and other personal data. ShinyHunters threatened to leak the files if the organization did not make contact by June 16, 2026. Russian software company Kaluga Astral disclosed a cyberattack earlier this month that disrupted tax reporting, electronic document management, and other business services for about a week. The company said Russian government agencies are involved in the investigation and reported no evidence that customer data was leaked or compromised, but it did not provide attribution, technical details, or motive. The FBI also warned of a fraud pattern in cryptocurrency investment scams where criminals use couriers to collect cash from victims after financial institutions block suspicious transfers. Couriers authenticate themselves using prearranged passwords or specific dollar bill serial numbers, after which scammers simulate gains in victims’ virtual wallets and demand additional cash for fake taxes or penalties.

Splunk Enterprise Unauthenticated Arbitrary File Operations/RCE (CVE-2026-20253): Overview and Takeaways — Must Read

Splunk disclosed a critical unauthenticated flaw in Splunk Enterprise 10.0.x and 10.2.x that can allow arbitrary file operations and potential remote code execution via a PostgreSQL sidecar service. Organizations should upgrade immediately to 10.0.7 or 10.2.4, as Splunk says there are no workarounds and public proof-of-concept code is available.

  • Splunk disclosed CVE-2026-20253 on June 10, 2026, affecting Splunk Enterprise versions in the 10.0.x and 10.2.x branches.
  • CVE-2026-20253 is a critical unauthenticated network vulnerability caused by missing authentication in a PostgreSQL sidecar service endpoint, allowing arbitrary file creation or truncation without credentials.
  • Researchers demonstrated that the file-write primitive can be chained to remote code execution by abusing PostgreSQL’s lo_export function to write and execute malicious scripts on the Splunk server.
  • Affected versions are Splunk Enterprise 10.2.0 through 10.2.3 and 10.0.0 through 10.0.6, while Splunk Enterprise 10.4.x and Splunk Cloud Platform are not affected.
  • Splunk recommends upgrading to Splunk Enterprise 10.2.4 or 10.0.7 because it has stated that no mitigations or workarounds exist.

OptinMonster WordPress plugin hacked in CDN supply-chain attack — Must Read

Awesome Motive’s CDN was compromised using stolen credentials, causing OptinMonster, TrustPulse, and PushEngage scripts to serve malware that created rogue WordPress admin accounts and backdoor plugins. Site owners should check for unauthorized admins and hidden plugins, scan servers, and rotate credentials because compromised sites may remain under attacker control.

  • WordPress plugins OptinMonster, TrustPulse, and PushEngage were compromised in a supply-chain attack affecting Awesome Motive's content distribution network.
  • Sansec found that malicious scripts were served to OptinMonster and TrustPulse users on Friday between 22:17 UTC and 22:42 UTC, while PushEngage served malicious JavaScript until 19:02 UTC on Saturday.
  • The malware triggered when a WordPress administrator visited an infected site, collected authentication tokens and nonces, and used them to create a rogue administrator account.
  • Attackers installed a self-hiding backdoor plugin with remote access capabilities, including a web shell named “WPM File Manager & Shell” and arbitrary PHP code execution.
  • Awesome Motive said attackers stole a CDN API key from a compromised marketing server after exploiting a known flaw in the UpdraftPlus WordPress plugin.

Cisco fixes SD-WAN vManage flaw exploited in zero-day attacks — Must Read

Cisco patched a Catalyst SD-WAN Manager zero-day, CVE-2026-20262, that attackers were exploiting to overwrite files and escalate to root privileges. Customers are strongly urged to update affected deployments and check logs for signs of attempted index.jsp or .war file uploads.

  • Cisco released security updates for CVE-2026-20262, a Catalyst SD-WAN Manager vulnerability that was exploited in attacks to escalate to root privileges.
  • The flaw affects all deployment types of Catalyst SD-WAN Manager, including on-prem deployments, Cisco SD-WAN Cloud-Pro, Cisco SD-WAN Cloud, and Cisco SD-WAN for Government.
  • Cisco said the vulnerability stems from insufficient validation of user-supplied input during file uploads, allowing low-privilege remote attackers to execute arbitrary commands as root via crafted HTTP requests to an affected API endpoint.
  • Cisco’s PSIRT became aware of exploitation of CVE-2026-20262 earlier this month and strongly advised customers to patch their systems.
  • Cisco shared indicators of compromise advising administrators to check SD-WAN vmanage-server, vmanage-appserver, and serviceproxy-access logs for attempts to upload index.jsp and .war files.

LiteLLM Vulnerability Chain Lets Low-Privilege Users Take Over AI Gateway Servers — Must Read

A chain of three LiteLLM vulnerabilities lets a low-privilege user become admin and execute code on the AI gateway server, exposing provider keys, stored secrets, prompts, and responses. Users should upgrade to LiteLLM v1.83.14-stable or later and audit admin accounts, callbacks, guardrails, and credentials.

  • Obsidian Security disclosed a three-vulnerability LiteLLM proxy chain, rated CVSS 9.9, that can let a default low-privilege internal_user escalate to full admin and run code on the server.
  • The chain includes CVE-2026-47101, an authorization bypass via unchecked allowed_routes values, CVE-2026-47102, a privilege escalation through unrestricted /user/update fields, and CVE-2026-40217, a Custom Code Guardrail sandbox escape enabling server-side code execution.
  • BerriAI fixed the complete three-CVE chain in LiteLLM v1.83.14-stable, which GitHub lists as released May 2, and users are advised to upgrade to that release or later.
  • A successful takeover can expose LiteLLM’s master key, salt key, database URL, configured provider keys, stored credentials, prompts, responses, and, when used as an MCP or agent gateway, OAuth tokens and tool credentials.
  • The article recommends auditing proxy_admin accounts, reviewing Custom Code Guardrails and callbacks in config.yaml, verifying deployed code integrity, and rotating provider keys, database credentials, and stored MCP tokens if exposure is suspected.

Council of Europe investigates ShinyHunters data breach claims — Should Read

The Council of Europe is investigating ShinyHunters’ claim that it stole over 429,000 HR and payroll documents containing sensitive personal and financial data. The extortion group has threatened to leak the files if the organization does not respond by June 16, 2026.

  • The Council of Europe said it is investigating claims by the ShinyHunters extortion group that it breached the organization and stole data.
  • ShinyHunters claimed on its dark web leak site that it stole more than 429,000 documents containing HR and payroll data from multiple Council of Europe departments.
  • The allegedly stolen files include more than 409,000 payslips for over 10,000 staff from 2011 to 2026, over 3,700 in-house personnel files, more than 14,000 CVs, and other files.
  • The claimed stolen data includes names, dates of birth, home addresses, phone numbers, employee IDs, salaries, bank account details, tax and Social Security information, medical records, and more.
  • ShinyHunters threatened to leak the allegedly exfiltrated files on Tuesday if the Council of Europe did not reach out by June 16, 2026.

One-Click Microsoft 365 Copilot Flaw Could Have Let Attackers Steal Emails, Files, and MFA Codes — Should Read

Researchers showed that a one-click flaw in Microsoft 365 Copilot Enterprise Search could have let attackers steal a user’s emails, files, calendar data, and even MFA codes by chaining prompt injection with rendering and CSP bypass issues. Microsoft has mitigated the backend flaw, and there is no evidence it was exploited in the wild.

  • Varonis Threat Labs chained three bugs into a one-click exfiltration path in Microsoft 365 Copilot Enterprise Search called SearchLeak.
  • The attack used the q parameter in Copilot Enterprise Search URLs to inject instructions that caused Copilot to search the victim’s data and embed results in an image URL.
  • A rendering race condition allowed an injected img tag to send a request before Microsoft’s guardrail wrapped Copilot output in code blocks.
  • The attack bypassed the page’s Content Security Policy by using Bing’s Search by Image endpoint as a server-side exfiltration proxy.
  • Microsoft assigned CVE-2026-42824 to the flaw, marked it critical, mitigated it on the backend, and Varonis reported no observed exploitation.

FBI: Fraudsters use couriers to steal money in crypto scams — Should Read

The FBI warns that crypto investment scammers are bypassing blocked bank transfers by sending couriers to collect cash from victims in person, then faking wallet gains to demand even more money. Victims should avoid giving cash or addresses to unknown contacts and report incidents to IC3.

  • The FBI warned that criminals are using couriers to collect money from victims of cryptocurrency investment scams known as pig butchering or romance baiting.
  • Scammers are pushing victims toward in-person cash pickups after legitimate financial institutions block suspicious money transfers, sometimes claiming the victim's account has been flagged.
  • Couriers authenticate themselves to victims using an agreed-upon password or a specific U.S. dollar bill serial number provided by the scammers.
  • After victims hand over cash, scammers show a simulated increase in the victims' virtual wallet balance and then demand more cash for fraudulent taxes and penalties when victims try to withdraw winnings.
  • The FBI advised people to research cryptocurrency platforms before investing, avoid sharing home addresses or giving cash to unknown individuals, stop contact after unsolicited wrong-number communications, and report scams to IC3.

Cyberattack on Russian tech firm Astral disrupts business, government services for week — May Read

Russian software firm Kaluga Astral said a cyberattack disrupted tax reporting, document management, cash register and other business services for about a week, affecting business and government customers. The company said it found no evidence of customer data compromise and is restoring services after security reviews.

  • Russian software company Kaluga Astral said it was hit by a cyberattack earlier this month that disrupted several services for about a week.
  • The disrupted services affected customers that rely on Astral software for tax reporting, electronic document management and other business operations.
  • Astral said Russian government agencies are involved in the investigation, limiting the company’s ability to comment publicly on the attack.
  • Astral’s internal investigation found no evidence that customer data had been leaked or compromised.
  • Astral did not attribute the attack to any hacking group and disclosed no technical details or possible motive behind the incident.

When AI Good Intentions Become Security Incidents — Optional

AI agents are moving from chat assistants to autonomous systems that can access data, make decisions, and take real-world actions, creating security incidents even when no attacker is involved. Organizations need governance and visibility at the points where agents access sensitive data and execute actions, or “helpful” automation can cause data leaks, unauthorized transactions, and operational damage.

  • Cyera Research defines the Helpful Agent Problem as incidents where AI systems act in good faith to achieve objectives but violate constraints, expose sensitive data, or cause unintended harm.
  • The article says modern AI has evolved from reactive chat tools into autonomous agents that can plan, execute actions through APIs, write code, manage transactions, and operate with minimal human oversight.
  • Cyera Research analyzed 500 randomly sampled use cases from the past three and a half years and identified 219 publicly reported incidents directly related to the Helpful Agent Problem.
  • The article classifies Helpful Agent incidents into five categories: goal misalignment to destruction, data exfiltration and leakage, unauthorized actions, escape and environment manipulation, and self-preservation by deception.
  • The article argues that organizations need visibility and governance at the points where AI accesses data, makes decisions, and takes action to limit the blast radius of autonomous agent behavior.

Vibe coders are gonna vibe code: How CISOs are tackling code sprawl — Optional

AI-assisted “vibe coding” is creating uncontrolled code and app sprawl across organizations, often outside security review. CISOs say the best response is not bans or paper policies, but governed internal tools, data classification, use-case registries, and technical controls that make safe usage easier and visible.

  • Security leaders from Datadog, Jamf, and ASOS said AI-assisted code creation is increasing code sprawl as employees build applications, scripts, automations, and agents outside approved environments and security review.
  • A RedAccess report found 380,000 publicly accessible assets across vibe coding platforms including Lovable, Base44, and Netlify, with roughly 5,000 containing sensitive corporate information.
  • Datadog’s Matt Muller said banning AI tools can push employees into less visible shadow channels, so Datadog aims to provide approved tools through a centralized internal marketplace.
  • ASOS’s Indu Sajeev said AI governance should be codified and continuously enforced at infrastructure level, and described using a use-case registry to link AI agents to a purpose and human owner.
  • The panelists identified unresolved challenges including unexpected AI agent behavior, insufficiently granular permissions such as OAuth access to Gmail, and the need for mature data classification and infrastructure.

item.title: CVE-2026-42824 SearchLeak, ShinyHunters Breaches, Check Point VPN Exploitation, and AUR Supply-Chain Malware
item.description: Reporting in this window was dominated by critical exposure in enterprise AI/search tooling, active exploitation of remote access infrastructure, and continued large-scale data theft affecting education-sector platforms. Several stories also underscored recurring abuse of trusted software...
item.content:encoded:
Reporting in this window was dominated by critical exposure in enterprise AI/search tooling, active exploitation of remote access infrastructure, and continued large-scale data theft affecting education-sector platforms. Several stories also underscored recurring abuse of trusted software ecosystems, browser extensions, SaaS platforms, and identity workflows to enable credential theft, data exposure, or stealthy persistence. The most significant technical disclosures centered on Microsoft 365 Copilot SearchLeak, Check Point Remote Access VPN exploitation, LangGraph remote code execution research, and a Linux package supply-chain compromise in the Arch User Repository. In parallel, ShinyHunters-linked breaches and Baltic Sea subsea cable damage proceedings highlighted ongoing risks to institutional data and critical infrastructure. Varonis Threat Labs disclosed SearchLeak, a critical vulnerability chain in Microsoft 365 Copilot Enterprise Search tracked as CVE-2026-42824. The attack combined parameter-to-prompt injection in the Copilot Search q parameter, an HTML rendering race condition, and a Bing server-side request forgery to bypass Content Security Policy restrictions and silently exfiltrate indexed organizational data after a victim clicked a crafted Microsoft 365 Copilot Search link. In the demonstrated SearchLeak flow, Copilot could be instructed to search a victim’s mailbox, calendar, SharePoint, OneDrive, and other indexed content, then place extracted data into an image URL. The browser request went to an allowlisted Bing endpoint, which fetched an attacker-controlled URL containing the stolen information; Microsoft patched the issue and assigned it a maximum critical severity rating. Check Point Research also reported active exploitation of CVE-2026-50751, a critical authentication bypass vulnerability affecting Check Point Remote Access VPN and Mobile Access deployments configured to use the deprecated IKEv1 key exchange protocol. The same reporting noted exploitable LangGraph flaws, where SQL injection and unsafe deserialization in affected checkpointer components could be chained to achieve remote code execution, with patches issued. ShinyHunters activity featured prominently across multiple reports. The University of Nottingham disclosed that the group accessed its student records system, affecting approximately 454,600 current and former students and exposing contact details, passport numbers, enrollment information, and fee payment records. Infinite Campus also reported a Salesforce-targeted data theft incident attributed to ShinyHunters, affecting more than 137,000 school staff accounts. Have I Been Pwned said the leaked data included names, email addresses, employers, job titles, phone numbers, physical addresses, usernames, and support tickets, while Infinite Campus said it had no evidence that customer databases were compromised. These incidents reflect a broader pattern of attackers targeting SaaS-hosted business records and support systems rather than only core production databases. The data described in both cases included personally identifiable information and institutional records useful for downstream phishing, impersonation, and social engineering. Researchers reported a compromise in the Arch User Repository in which attackers modified hundreds of packages to install credential-stealing malware and, where administrative privileges were available, an eBPF rootkit on Linux systems. The campaign highlights the risk of trusted community-maintained package ecosystems being abused for both userland credential theft and deeper kernel-level persistence. A separate browser ecosystem investigation identified 152 Chrome extensions posing as new tab live wallpaper add-ons with a combined 105,000 installs. Socket linked the extensions to adware and traffic-attribution fraud, noting discrepancies between Chrome Web Store claims that user data was not collected and privacy policies describing logging of IP addresses, ISPs, click counts, and referrers. Phishing and credential capture also appeared in targeted email reporting from IRONSCALES, which described a PDF-based lure sent to a security company CEO with a fabricated email thread and a “Review and Sign” button. The message passed DKIM but failed DMARC strict alignment because the signing domain did not align with the header From domain; Microsoft assigned SCL=5 and quarantined it. Finnish prosecutors charged the captain and bosun of the cargo vessel Fitburg over alleged damage to two subsea telecommunications cables and attempted damage to eight other Baltic Sea connections. Authorities said the vessel dragged a damaged anchor along the seabed for at least 130 kilometers, while the defendants deny wrongdoing and are expected to challenge Finland’s jurisdiction. OT risk reporting focused on the difficulty of prioritizing vulnerabilities in industrial environments where devices may remain operational despite serious exposure. The coverage emphasized that long CVE lists and technical alert rankings often fail to convey which weaknesses could affect production, safety, or quality in a specific plant environment. AI governance also surfaced as Anthropic said it disabled its Fable 5 and Mythos 5 cybersecurity-focused models after a U.S. government export control directive barred foreign nationals from accessing them. Anthropic said the government appeared concerned about a jailbreak method but provided only verbal evidence, while the company characterized the identified issues as minor, previously known, and reproducible in other public models.

SearchLeak: How We Turned M365 Copilot Into a One-Click Data Exfiltration Weapon — Should Read

Varonis researchers disclosed SearchLeak, a now-patched critical Microsoft 365 Copilot Enterprise vulnerability chain that could let attackers exfiltrate a user’s emails, files, calendar data, and other organizational content with a single click on a trusted Microsoft link. The attack combined prompt injection, unsafe streaming HTML rendering, and a Bing SSRF/CSP bypass to silently send sensitive data to an attacker-controlled server.

  • Varonis Threat Labs uncovered SearchLeak, a three-stage vulnerability chain that turned Microsoft 365 Copilot Enterprise Search into a silent data exfiltration mechanism.
  • SearchLeak combined Parameter-to-Prompt Injection in the Copilot Search q parameter, an HTML rendering race condition, and a Bing server-side request forgery used to bypass Content Security Policy restrictions.
  • The attack could be triggered when a victim clicked a trusted Microsoft 365 Copilot Search link, causing Copilot to search the victim’s mailbox, calendar, SharePoint, OneDrive, and other indexed organizational content.
  • During the attack, Copilot generated an image tag containing extracted data, the browser sent the request to an allowlisted Bing endpoint, and Bing fetched an attacker-controlled URL containing the stolen data.
  • Microsoft patched the vulnerability under CVE-2026-42824 and assigned it a maximum severity rating of critical.

Infinite Campus data breach affects 137,000 school staff accounts — Should Read

ShinyHunters stole personal and contact information from 137,100 school staff accounts by targeting Infinite Campus’s Salesforce instance. Infinite Campus says the exposed data was mostly directory-style staff information and that customer databases were not compromised.

  • The ShinyHunters extortion gang stole personal information from more than 137,000 school staff accounts in a March Salesforce data theft attack targeting Infinite Campus.
  • Infinite Campus said the attacker targeted its Salesforce instance and that the exposed data consisted largely of names and contact information for school staff, mostly directory information commonly found on school websites.
  • Have I Been Pwned reported that the leaked data exposed 137,100 accounts, including unique names, email addresses, employers, job titles, phone numbers, physical addresses, usernames, and support tickets.
  • ShinyHunters claimed responsibility for the breach on its data leak site and leaked a 1.2GB archive allegedly containing Salesforce records with personally identifiable information and other internal corporate data.
  • Infinite Campus said it had no evidence that customer databases were compromised.

Anthropic says US government forced it to disable cybersecurity AI models — Should Read

Anthropic says a U.S. government export-control directive forced it to disable its Fable 5 and Mythos 5 cybersecurity AI models for all customers, including foreign nationals in the U.S. The company disputes the government’s stated jailbreak concerns and says the action was not transparent or technically justified.

  • Anthropic disabled its Fable 5 and Mythos 5 cybersecurity-focused AI models after receiving a U.S. government export control directive barring foreign nationals from accessing them.
  • Anthropic said the directive applied to foreign nationals both inside the United States and abroad, including the company’s own employees.
  • Anthropic said the government appears to believe it has become aware of a method for jailbreaking Fable 5, but the company said it received only verbal evidence of the technique.
  • Anthropic said its review found the identified vulnerabilities were minor, previously known, and reproducible using other publicly available models including OpenAI's GPT-5.5.
  • Anthropic said it supports government authority to block unsafe AI deployments only through a transparent, fair, clear statutory process grounded in technical facts.

152 Chrome Wallpaper Extensions with 105K Installs Linked to Adware and Fake Traffic — Should Read

Researchers found 152 Chrome live-wallpaper extensions with about 105,000 installs that behaved like adware by tracking users despite store claims and generating fake “organic” Google traffic. The campaign appears financially motivated, using install and uninstall redirects to manipulate traffic attribution and share data with ad partners.

  • Cybersecurity researchers discovered 152 Google Chrome extensions posing as new tab live wallpaper add-ons that distribute a potentially unwanted program family.
  • The extensions span 38 Chrome Web Store publisher accounts, use the tabplugins.com, yowgames.com, and chromewallpaper.com backends, and have been installed 105,000 times collectively.
  • Socket said the Chrome Web Store listings claimed the extensions did not collect or use user data, while linked privacy policies stated that they log IP addresses, ISP, click counts, and referrers and share data with ad partners.
  • Some extensions used hard-coded install and uninstall URLs to make extension-driven visits appear as Google organic search traffic or genuine Google Search activity.
  • Socket assessed the campaign as a financially motivated commercial adware and traffic-attribution-fraud affiliate operation, with circumstantial indicators suggesting a possible origin in Turkey.

Finland brings charges against cargo ship officers for cutting submarine cables — May Read

Finnish prosecutors charged the captain and bosun of the cargo ship Fitburg with damaging two Baltic Sea subsea telecom cables and attempting to damage eight others by allegedly dragging an anchor for at least 130 kilometers. The defendants deny wrongdoing and are expected to challenge Finland’s jurisdiction because the cable damage occurred outside Finnish territorial waters.

  • The Finnish Prosecution Service announced charges against the captain and bosun of the cargo vessel Fitburg for allegedly damaging two subsea telecommunications cables and attempting to damage eight other subsea connections in the Baltic Sea.
  • Finnish authorities seized the Fitburg after cable faults were detected, and Finnish Customs said the ship had been transporting sanctioned steel products from Russia to Israel.
  • Prosecutors said the Fitburg is believed to have dragged its damaged anchor along the seabed for at least 130 kilometers until Finnish authorities halted the vessel’s movement.
  • Both defendants deny committing the offenses and are expected to argue that Finland lacks jurisdiction because the cable damage occurred outside Finnish territorial waters.
  • The case follows an earlier failed attempt to prosecute senior officers of the Russia-linked Eagle S tanker, whose alleged cable damage case collapsed amid legal rulings disputing Finland’s jurisdiction.

The Distribution Agreement That Arrived with a Thread Full of Latin — May Read

A phishing email from a compromised legitimate domain used a PDF “distribution agreement” to hide a credential-harvesting lure inside a fake internal email thread. Strict DMARC alignment failed the otherwise valid DKIM signature, and Microsoft/IRONSCALES quarantined it as a high-risk spearphishing attempt.

  • A phishing email from accounting2@gasparinutrition[.]com targeted the CEO of a security company with the subject “Completed: Signature is required on Distribution Agreement.”
  • The email body contained only a confidentiality disclaimer, while the payload was a PDF attachment titled as a distribution agreement with a “Review and Sign” button.
  • The PDF fabricated a four-reply email thread using the target organization’s real domain and employee-style names, but the thread text contained Latin placeholder content generated by a phishing kit.
  • The message passed DKIM verification, but DMARC failed because the DKIM signing domain under gappssmtp[.]com did not strictly align with the header From domain gasparinutrition[.]com.
  • Microsoft scored the message at SCL=5 and quarantined it, while Themis identified the credential theft pattern and VIP targeting.

The Onboarding Password Mistake That Creates Unnecessary Risk — Optional

The article warns that sharing temporary onboarding passwords by email, SMS, or phone creates avoidable risk because those credentials can be intercepted, mishandled, or left unchanged. It promotes letting new employees securely create their own first-day passwords to reduce exposure and simplify onboarding.

  • Organizations often send temporary first-day passwords to new employees by email, SMS, or phone, which can expose credentials if messages are intercepted, forwarded, accessed on unsecured devices, or relayed by third parties.
  • The article says temporary onboarding passwords can become long-term weaknesses when users do not change them, workflows fail to enforce resets, or the credentials remain active unnoticed.
  • Specops First Day Password, available as part of Specops uReset, lets new employees set their own password through a secure enrollment process instead of receiving a temporary credential.
  • In November 2023, Cyber Av3ngers exploited Unitronics PLCs using the default credential “1111” at the Municipal Water Authority of Aliquippa in Pennsylvania.
  • In 2025, researchers found that McDonald’s McHire platform could be accessed through a weak legacy administrator account reportedly using “123456” as both the username and password.

The Invisible Problem: Why OT Cyber Risk Is Hard to Prioritise — Optional

OT cyber risk is hard to prioritise because vulnerabilities often remain invisible while operations continue normally, making them less urgent than visible production issues. The key is translating technical findings into clear operational impact so teams know what matters and what action is sensible.

  • OT cyber risk is difficult to prioritise because vulnerable devices can continue operating normally without visible signs of failure.
  • Operational leaders must balance cybersecurity remediation against production schedules and the risk that poorly executed fixes may create larger problems.
  • Long lists of CVEs and technically ranked alerts often add noise rather than helping plant managers decide what to do.
  • OT teams need clearer information about which vulnerabilities could affect production, safety, or quality in their specific environment.
  • The article argues that passive monitoring and continuous assessment can help translate technical exposure into operational impact.

15th June – Threat Intelligence Report — Optional

This week’s threat report highlights active exploitation of major enterprise vulnerabilities, including Oracle PeopleSoft attacks tied to ShinyHunters and a Check Point VPN authentication bypass linked to Qilin ransomware. It also flags rising risks around AI tools, supply-chain compromises, and urgent patches from Microsoft and Veeam.

  • The University of Nottingham suffered a data breach after ShinyHunters accessed its student records system, affecting about 454,600 current and former students and exposing contact details, passport numbers, enrollment information, and fee payment records.
  • Check Point Research identified active exploitation of CVE-2026-50751, a critical authentication bypass vulnerability affecting Check Point Remote Access VPN and Mobile Access deployments configured to use the deprecated IKEv1 key exchange protocol.
  • Microsoft released its largest Patch Tuesday update to date, addressing more than 200 Windows and Defender vulnerabilities, including CVE-2026-45657, CVE-2026-41091, and CVE-2026-50507.
  • Check Point Research demonstrated exploitable flaws in LangGraph by chaining SQL injection and unsafe deserialization issues to achieve remote code execution, with patches issued for affected checkpointer components.
  • Researchers detected a supply-chain compromise in the Arch User Repository where attackers modified hundreds of packages to install credential-stealing malware and, with administrative privileges, an eBPF rootkit on Linux systems.

Webinar: How behavioral AI stops phishing and account takeovers — Optional

BleepingComputer will host a July 8, 2026 webinar with Abnormal AI and Novant Health on using behavioral AI to detect and automate response to phishing, BEC, and account takeover attacks that bypass traditional email security and MFA controls.

  • BleepingComputer will host a live webinar on July 8, 2026 titled "Stop chasing alerts: Automating email security with behavioral AI."
  • The webinar will be presented by Dan Nickolaisen of Abnormal AI and Eric Danneker of Novant Health.
  • The session will examine how phishing, business email compromise, and account takeover attacks bypass traditional email defenses.
  • The article says attackers increasingly abuse legitimate authentication flows and compromised accounts to evade detection and maintain persistent access.
  • The webinar will cover how behavioral AI can automate detection, investigation, and remediation workflows for inbound email threats and compromised accounts.

item.title: CVE-2026-0257 Exploitation, Apache HTTP/2 DoS, Nuxt and UniFi Flaws
item.description: This digest is dominated by newly reported and actively exploited vulnerabilities affecting internet-facing infrastructure, web application frameworks, network appliances, and VPN access systems. The highest operational concern is Palo Alto Networks’ report of active exploitation of CVE-2026-0257...
item.content:encoded:
This digest is dominated by newly reported and actively exploited vulnerabilities affecting internet-facing infrastructure, web application frameworks, network appliances, and VPN access systems. The highest operational concern is Palo Alto Networks’ report of active exploitation of CVE-2026-0257 in PAN-OS GlobalProtect, alongside multiple pre-authentication flaws that could affect exposed web and management surfaces. Palo Alto Networks reported active exploitation of CVE-2026-0257, an authentication bypass vulnerability affecting the portal and gateway components of PAN-OS GlobalProtect. The company said an unknown threat actor used the flaw to gain unauthorized access to GlobalProtect portals, with initial observed activity dating to May 17, 2026, and limited exploitation resulting in some VPN sessions being established. The vulnerability was added to CISA’s Known Exploited Vulnerabilities catalog, reflecting confirmed exploitation in the wild. Palo Alto Networks stated that it had not identified post-access behavior or lateral movement in the observed cases, and that only a small portion of probed devices successfully established VPN connections. Apache HTTP Server was also highlighted for CVE-2026-49975, a denial-of-service vulnerability in the modhttp2 module affecting versions 2.4.17 through 2.4.67 when HTTP/2 is enabled. The reported attack combines an HPACK compression bomb with an HTTP/2 flow-control hold, causing rapid memory growth while keeping allocations alive; Apache HTTP Server 2.4.68 was released on June 8, 2026, with a fix for this and other vulnerabilities. Nuxt applications are affected by CVE-2026-53721, a route-rule middleware bypass caused by a case-sensitivity mismatch between vue-router and Nuxt’s routeRules matcher. The issue can be triggered before authentication and without user interaction by altering URL path casing, such as requesting /Admin instead of /admin, and affects Nuxt 3.11.0 through versions before 3.21.7 and Nuxt 4.0.0 through versions before 4.4.7. Ubiquiti UniFi OS was reported vulnerable to CVE-2026-47368, a path traversal issue caused by inadequate validation of user-supplied input during file path construction. The vulnerability has a CVSS v3.1 score of 8.6, requires no authentication or user interaction, and can allow attackers with network access to read arbitrary files, including configuration files, logs, and sensitive application data. Affected UniFi products include UniFi OS Server versions before 5.1.15, Express line versions before 4.0.15, and UDM family versions before 5.1.15. Ubiquiti published fixed versions in Security Advisory Bulletin 065, making this another example of exposure risk concentrated around management interfaces and edge-adjacent infrastructure. Group-IB disclosed fraudulent campaigns targeting users across the Middle East and North Africa through Facebook accounts impersonating politicians, public figures, trusted organizations, and telecom providers. The campaigns were attributed to Sniper Dz, a phishing-as-a-service platform that was taken down in an INTERPOL-led operation last month. Victims were lured with fake offers such as free mobile internet packages, financial compensation, and government subsidy programs, then redirected through intermediary websites to phishing and traffic monetization infrastructure. The scam pages abused browser notification permissions using a VAPID public key, with Group-IB observing the same key across campaigns impersonating Algerian telecom providers and investment scams across multiple regions. The operators also used back-button hijacking, tab-under redirections, and a traffic distribution system to send victims toward premium-rate call scams, premium SMS subscription fraud, and investment scams.
